macro_segments.title: Introduction and Overview: The Achan Narrative and Confession Theology

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on Daf 44 and the Mishnah's focus on viduy (confession) before execution
The theological basis for viduy: Achan's confession, atonement through death, and the role of Beis Din as God's agents
The dialectic between confession as personal atonement versus public clarity and acceptance of punishment
Does atonement require confession, and how does public acknowledgment parallel the hatra'ah (warning) requirement

macro_segments.title: The Five Types of Mo'al B'Charamim and the Timing of Achan's Punishment

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's enumeration of five instances of mo'al b'charamim (taking benefit from banned property) across the wilderness and conquest periods
The question of why Achan was not punished during Moshe's time despite sinning then, but only after crossing the Jordan
Rabbi Yehuda's explanation: collective punishment for hidden sins only applied after crossing the Jordan and entering national identity
Rabbi Nechemia's counter-position: communal responsibility for preventable public sins, not hidden ones
The distinction between national unity/responsibility versus individual blame; the shift from individualist to collective society
How Achan's family knowledge of his sin made it a quasi-public matter, triggering collective responsibility

macro_segments.title: Achan's Multiple Transgressions and the Depth of His Sinfulness

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Abba bar Zavda: Even after sinning, Israel retains its identity; the analogy of the hadas (myrtle) growing among thorns
Rabbi Ila's teaching that Achan transgressed all five books of Torah, derived from the five gam (also) words in the divine accusation
Achan's attempt to reverse his brit milah by stretching his foreskin; proof from the phrase 'avru et briti'
Rabbi Abba bar Zavda's midrash: Achan had intercourse with a na'arah me'orasah (betrothed maiden), proven by 'ki asah nevalah'
Ravina's explanation: Achan suffered sekilah like a na'arah me'orasah; both receive the same punishment

macro_segments.title: Collective Punishment of Achan's Family and the Purpose of Public Execution

micro_segments.title: The Reish Galuta's challenge to Rav Huna: Why were Achan's sons, daughters, and possessions killed if they did not sin?
The response: lirdosam—to beat down/educate the people; public execution serves to deter others and protect communal institutions
The Ramban's principle for disproportionate punishment: zaken mamre, edim zomemim, ben sorer u'moreh, navi sheker all require 'v'chol Yisrael yishme'u v'yira'u'
The justification of innocent deaths for institutional preservation and public message; tension with minimizing capital punishment

macro_segments.title: The Burning and Stoning of Achan and the Meaning of Double Punishment

micro_segments.title: Question: Why both burning (sreifa) and stoning (sekilah) when one should suffice for death?
Ravina's answer: Things designated for burning were burned; things designated for stoning were stoned—differentiation by penalty type

macro_segments.title: Achan's Confession and Joshua's Challenge to God

micro_segments.title: Achan's confession of what he saw and took: a fine cloak from Shinar, two hundred shekels of silver, and a bar of gold
Rav's and Shmuel's identification of the garment: either an embroidered silk cloak or a fine wool/dyed garment
Rav Nachman: Achan poured out (vayatzikum) the stolen items before God, which Rav reinterprets as 'slammed them down' (chavtam)
Rav Nachman's reading: Joshua, not Achan, did this; Joshua challenged God, asking why thirty-six people died for one man's sin
Rav Nechemia's counter: Both Pinchas and Moses also challenged God; Pinchas when twenty-four thousand died; Moses asking why the Jordan was crossed

macro_segments.title: God's Response to Joshua and the Question of Joshua's Own Responsibility

micro_segments.title: God tells Joshua to get up (kum lach); Shila Darash: Joshua's sin was harsher than the people's—he created the cherem (ban)
Joshua was supposed to erect the altar-stones right after crossing the Jordan, not wait sixty mil to Har Gerizim and Har Eval
Tosafot's resolution through the Yerushalmi: either Joshua made two small mounds immediately, or reached Har Gerizim in one day
Rav's rebuttal via an amora: Joshua did exactly what he was commanded; there was no sin of Joshua, but he contributed to the outcome
The implicit lesson from God's words and the closing of the account: don't place the spoils of Ai under cherem either

macro_segments.title: Joshua's Encounter with the Angel of God and the Primacy of Talmud Torah

micro_segments.title: When Joshua was near Jericho, he met an ish (person) standing before him who announced himself as sar tzeva Hashem (commander of God's army)
Joshua's concern: Can you greet someone at night? The halacha prohibits because it might be a demon; one cannot invoke God's name on a demon
The angel's self-identification reassures; demons will not say God's name, establishing the figure as neither demon nor fully human
The angel rebukes Joshua: Last night you omitted the tamid (daily sacrifice); now you are maintaining the siege at night instead of studying
Joshua asks which sin prompted the angel's visit; the angel answers: neglect of Talmud Torah—greater than omitting the daily sacrifice
Joshua immediately corrected by sleeping in the valley and ensuring the troops studied Torah deeply that night
Rav Shmuel bar Unya: Talmud Torah is greater than bringing daily sacrifices, explicitly taught by the angel's priority

macro_segments.title: Midrash on Debate and Secrets: Yechezkel's Rebuke and the Limits of Criticism

micro_segments.title: Abaye's question to Rav Dimi: How is the verse 'al taitzei l'riv maher' (don't quickly enter into a dispute) applied in Eretz Yisrael?
Rav Dimi's midrash: When God told Yechezkel to rebuke Israel as children of an Emori and Hittite, Yechezkel protested the shame it would bring to Abraham and Sarah
The principle: 'Fight your dispute with a person, but do not reveal another's secret'—do not embarrass one's ancestors to prove your point

The angel (identified as Gavriel or a spirit of judgment) has three names: Piscon (one who cuts words before God), Itmon (one who closes up Israel's sins), Sigron (one whose closure cannot be reopened)

macro_segments.title: The Power of Prayer and Abraham's Intercession for Israel

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Elazar: One should always pray before calamity strikes (yakdim adam tefillah latzarah)
Abraham's example: Had he not built an altar and prayed between Beis El and Ai, far more than thirty-six would have died there
Reish Lakish's reading: One who exerts themselves in prayer from below has no adversaries from above
Yochanan's addition: One should always pray for mercy that all heavenly forces strengthen you and no one stands against you

topical_tags.term: Mo'al b'charamim (taking benefit from banned property)
V'chol Yisrael yishme'u v'yira'u (and all Israel will hear and fear)
Angel / Sar tzeva Hashem (commander of God's army)
Secrets should not be revealed in dispute
